behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: This tests the contract of what gets sent to the API. Mocking the API client and inspecting the call arguments is sufficient to verify all taxonomy labels are included. No I/O or real API needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Call the classification function with a valid document. Assert that the mock was called with arguments containing the complete taxonomy label list (e.g., {'labels': ['legal', 'technical', 'financial', 'other']}).

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is input validation/sanitization after receiving an API response. A mock API returning an out-of-taxonomy label will reliably test the module's response-handling logic. No real API needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not in the taxonomy (e.g., 'unknown_category'). Call the classification function. Assert the module returns the string 'unknown' rather than forwarding the invalid label.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: This tests whether results are cached. A mock API with a deterministic return value will show if the module caches: call the classification function twice with the same document, and verify the mocked API is called exactly once (cache hit on second call) and both results are identical. MOCK-MISS: Could a mock miss this? No—if caching is broken, the mock call count will be 2, not 1. However, concurrency bugs or cache invalidation under stress would require Integration/System testing.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a deterministic label. Call the classification function twice with the same document input. Assert (1) both calls return the same result, and (2) the mocked API was called exactly once, proving the result was cached on the second call.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Could a pure in-process mock miss this failure? Yes. If we mock the API to report a fixed token count, the test passes but the real failure—a typical document actually exceeding the budget when tokenized—remains hidden. The real failure requires actual tokenization of the document (e.g., using tiktoken or the real tokenizer the module uses), not a mocked count. You need a real tokenizer library to catch this.
behaviors.test_description: Use the actual tokenizer library that the module depends on (e.g., tiktoken) to encode a typical document. Call the classification function with that document. Assert the module reports token consumption ≤ the configured per-call budget. Verify no artificial limits are violated.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Unit testing with a mocked API and mocked time.sleep can verify that retry logic exists (the code path for handling rate-limit errors is taken). However, real failures in backoff strategy (wrong wait duration, exponential backoff not applied, retry count limits, or actual timing behavior) would be hidden by mocking. To catch real timing/backoff failures, you need a test that exercises actual timing or simulates realistic retry scenarios with a real or semi-real API. MOCK-HIDE: A pure mock that returns rate-limit then success hides whether the backoff strategy is correct.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a rate-limit error (429 or similar) on the first call and a valid classification result on the second call. Mock time.sleep (or use freezegun) to avoid actual delays in the test. Call the classification function and assert (1) it does not immediately return an error, (2) it retries after the rate-limit, and (3) it returns the valid result from the retry. Consider also testing max retry limits and backoff timing where feasible.
